title: Overflow and containment

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Overflow and containment; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Overflow and containment
sources.claim: Overflow controls clipping and scroll containers when content exceeds a box, while wrapping and min-size choices determine whether overflow occurs.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Overflow and containment
sources.claim: Overflow controls clipping and scroll containers when content exceeds a box, while wrapping and min-size choices determine whether overflow occurs.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Overflow controls clipping and scroll containers when content exceeds a box, while wrapping and min-size choices determine whether overflow occurs.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A code sample uses overflow:auto and remains keyboard-scrollable without clipping long lines permanently. Your goal is to repair overflow across narrow, zoomed and long-content scenarios. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Rules, declarations and values; Intrinsic sizing and constraints; Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Overflow controls clipping and scroll containers when content exceeds a box, while wrapping and min-size choices determine whether overflow occurs. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Hidden content can become inaccessible, scrollbars affect layout, focus can move into clipped regions, and two-axis scrolling harms reading. This draft therefore rejects: overflow:hidden is a safe universal fix for any layout that becomes too large.

includes: Handle long words, scroll regions and clipping without hiding essential content or focus.
Repair overflow across narrow, zoomed and long-content scenarios.
excludes: Hidden content can become inaccessible, scrollbars affect layout, focus can move into clipped regions, and two-axis scrolling harms reading.

outcomes: Repair overflow across narrow, zoomed and long-content scenarios.

examples.title: Overflow and containment: concrete case
examples.situation: A code sample uses overflow:auto and remains keyboard-scrollable without clipping long lines permanently.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A code sample uses overflow:auto and remains keyboard-scrollable without clipping long lines permanently.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Resolve the sizing cause first and use accessible scrolling where content must remain available; do not hide meaningful overflow blindly.
examples.result: The result follows because Resolve the sizing cause first and use accessible scrolling where content must remain available; do not hide meaningful overflow blindly.

examples.title: Overflow and containment: boundary case
examples.situation: Hidden content can become inaccessible, scrollbars affect layout, focus can move into clipped regions, and two-axis scrolling harms reading.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Hidden content can become inaccessible, scrollbars affect layout, focus can move into clipped regions, and two-axis scrolling harms reading.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: overflow:hidden is a safe universal fix for any layout that becomes too large.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Resolve the sizing cause first and use accessible scrolling where content must remain available; do not hide meaningful overflow blindly.

misconceptions.correction: Resolve the sizing cause first and use accessible scrolling where content must remain available; do not hide meaningful overflow blindly.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: overflow:hidden is a safe universal fix for any layout that becomes too large.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Overflow and containment, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Overflow and containment before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to repair overflow across narrow, zoomed and long-content scenarios. Keep this boundary in view: Hidden content can become inaccessible, scrollbars affect layout, focus can move into clipped regions, and two-axis scrolling harms reading.
